EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was observed five miles north of St. Thomas.

Tropical wave, former tropical depression number 5, crossed the U.S. Virgin Islands and produced heavy rains. Urban flooding was reported along the north shore road to Estate Le Grande Princess and at Sunny Isle. Urban flooding was also reported in Western St. Croix at Mon Bijou and Estate Calhoun.  These rains represented a relief to the dry spell that prevailed during the previous part of July, when the drought stress hurted vegetation, plant and livestock farmers. Bruh fires in St. Croix consumed many acres of grazing land.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A waterspout was reported 14 miles southeast of St. Thomas.
The National Park Service in St. John reported ground swells in excess of nine feet across the northern beaches with some beaches closed due to flooding.
A funnel cloud was reported by a pilot 50 miles south of St. Thomas moving west.
V.I. Radio reported a boat washed ashore on Inner Brass Island along the north coast of St. Thomas.
A storm spotter from St. Croix reported one to three inches of standing water on road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was reported in Gallows Bay St. Croix.
The Virgin Islands Radio reported a waterspout north of St. Thomas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mariner reported a waterspout south of the island of St. John.
A mariner reported a waterspout just west of Christmas Cove in St. James Island.
A pilot reported a waterspout 15 miles northwest of St. Croix.
Three waterspouts were reported 20 miles southeast of St. Thomas.
A pilot reported an ash cloud from St. Croix to Vieques at an altitude of 9 to 10 thousand feet.
Several pilots reported an extremely thick volcanic ash cloud between 19 and 22 thousand feet.
A funnel cloud was reported one mile south of the air traffic control tower.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms crossed St. Croix producing strong wind gusts. A storm spotter in Christiansted reported gusts of 50 mph at Maria Hill. No damage reports were received.
A mariner reported a waterspout north of Tortola near Guano Island.
Heavy rains were reported over the west end of St. Thomas. Minor flooding occurred at Point Nadir and Charlotte Amalie.
Heavy rains were reported in St. Thomas.

Showers and thunderstorms associated with a tropical wave moved over St. Croix producing periods of heavy rain that continued across the U.S. Virgin Islands through Monday. The heavy rains produced flooding of poorly drained areas, power failures, and telephone service disruptions in St. Croix. Around two inches of rain were recorded in Fredericksted between Sunday night and Monday morning.
Several waterspouts were reported in the  coastal waters near the Virgin Islands between 740 to 830 AST.
Several waterspouts were reported in the  coastal waters near the Virgin Islands between 740 to 830 AST.
Several waterspouts were reported in the  coastal waters near the Virgin Islands between 740 to 830 AST.

A tropical wave produced moderate rainfall and occasional heavy rains  across  the U.S. Virgin Islands for most of the day.  In St Thomas  urban flooding  were reported in low lying areas  near  Smith Bay,  the entry of Renaissance Grand Hotel, Havensite, and Cancryn School.  VITEMA  reported  urban flooding in St. Croix. near Castle Burke and other low lying areas and at least two downed trees.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A gale center located north of Hispaniola, in combination, with a low pressure over the Western Caribbean and a tropical wave across the Eastern Caribbean combined to produce unstable weather across the U.S.V.I.  Strong southwest winds were common during this period.  Unofficial reports from a St. Croix resident at Marion hoj 25 indicated strong winds from the 13th trough the 15th.  The highest recorded unofficial wind speed were on the 15th with 1 minute sustained  winds of 30 mph and gusts to 47 mph from the  south southwest at 1420 AST.  These winds produced high seas across the U.S. Virgin Islands coastal waters. Two charter boats, one dive boat, and six sail boats sunk at the Frederick harbour in St. Croix.
